verses.text: Asaph et Jeduthun ont proclamé les messages de Dieu, ce qui signifie qu’ils étaient prophètes ainsi que musiciens.
Asaph et Jeduthun ont proclamé les messages de Dieu, ce qui signifie qu’ils étaient prophètes ainsi que musiciens.

25:4 Of Heman: the sons of Heman,.... The third chief singer: Bukkiah, Mattaniah, Uzziel; the same with Azareel, 1 Chr 25:18. Shebuel; called Shubael, 1 Chr 25:20. and Jerimoth, Hananiah, Hanani, Eliathah, Giddalti, and Romamtiezer, Joshbekashah, Mallothi, Hothir, and Mahazioth; in all fourteen, to whom came the sixth, ninth, eleventh, fifteenth, sixteenth, eighteenth, twentieth, twenty second, twenty fourth, seventeenth, nineteenth, twentieth, and twenty third lots, 1 Chr 25:13.

25:5 Heman the king's seer—The title of "seer" or "prophet of David" is also given to Gad (1 Chr 21:9), and to Jeduthun (2 Chr 29:14-15), in the words (Margin, "matters") of God. to lift up the horn—that is, to blow loudly in the worship of God; or perhaps it means nothing more than that he presided over the wind instruments, as Jeduthun over the harp. Heman had been appointed at first to serve at Gibeon (1 Chr 16:41). But his destination seems to have been changed at a subsequent period.

25:6 All these were under the hands of their father—Asaph had four sons, Jeduthun six, and Heman fourteen, equal to twenty-four; making the musicians with their brethren the singers, an amount of two hundred eighty-eight. For, like the priests and Levites, they were divided into twenty-four courses of twelve men each, equal to two hundred eighty-eight, who served a week in rotation; and these, half of whom officiated every week with a proportionate number of assistants, were skilful and experienced musicians, capable of leading and instructing the general musical corps, which comprised no less than four thousand (1 Chr 23:5).

25:7 So the number of them, with their brethren,.... Who each of them had twelve with them, as after reckoned: that were instructed in the songs of the Lord; taught how to sing them: even all that were cunning; expert and well skilled in the science of singing: was two hundred and eighty eight; for there being twenty four classes answerable to those of the priests, and twelve in each class, twelve times twenty four makes two hundred and eighty eight.
Comme pour les prêtres, l’ordre des musiciens par familles était décidé par tirage au sort sacré pour écarter toute préférence (voir **24.5** , **31** ).

25:9 Now the first lot came forth for Asaph to Joseph,.... His second son to the preference of the firstborn, 1 Chr 25:2, the second to Gedaliah, who with his brethren and sons were twelve: and which must also be understood of Joseph, and supplied to him; for those being both in one verse, as Jarchi observes, it is but once mentioned, but must be supplied, or otherwise the number of two hundred and eighty eight could not be made up;

25:10 The third to Zaccur, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve: The lot-cast assignment for the third musical course goes to Zaccur, Asaph's firstborn (cp. 25:2). Each course consisted of twelve men — the family head plus his sons and brothers — matching the twelve-tribe symmetry that pervades temple administration (cp. 1 Kgs 18:31).

25:11 The fourth to Izri, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve: The fourth course belonged to Izri (also spelled Zeri in 25:3), one of Jeduthun's six sons. The careful enumeration honored each family equally — no son of Heman, Asaph, or Jeduthun took precedence by birth order; lot-casting placed them as the Lord chose (cp. Prov 16:33).
25:12 The fifth to Nethaniah - The third son of Asaph. Thus we find the lot did not run in any particular kind of order.

25:13 The sixth to Bukkiah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve: The casting of lots for the twenty-four musical courses (25:9-31) gives every family equal share. Each course numbered twelve men; the symmetry suggests order, fairness, and a deliberate yearlong rotation matching the festival calendar.
25:14 Jesharelah - Supposed to be the same with Uzziel, son of Heman.

25:15 The eighth to Jeshaiah: One of the twenty-four divisions established by lot. The Hebrew *goral* (lot) names a method that bypassed human preference and committed assignment to God (cp. Prov 16:33). Even worship's rota was placed under divine choice.

25:16 The ninth to Mattaniah: The list moves through Heman's, Asaph's, and Jeduthun's sons in sequence, the casting following the order of the lots' draw. The careful numbering — 1st, 2nd, 3rd, on through 24th — is the same pattern Luke recalls in the priestly courses (Luke 1:5, 8-9).

25:17 The tenth to Shimei: The lot-system established a rotating schedule that allowed every Levitical singer to share leadership across the year. No one family monopolized the music; equity in worship was structurally ensured.

25:18 The eleventh to Azareel: The continuing list. Azareel means 'God has helped' — a fitting name for one set apart to lead Israel's praise. The Chronicler preserves each singer's name because each was personally consecrated, not interchangeable.

25:19 The twelfth to Hashabiah: Half the divisions are now named. The careful tally — twelve down, twelve to go — patterns the temple's worship after twelve tribes, twelve months, and the broader symbolism of completeness (cp. Rev 21:12-14).

25:20 The thirteenth to Shubael: The second half of the list opens. Shubael (var. Shebuel) is named earlier as a son of Heman (25:4); his name means 'return to God' and fits the singers' calling to lead the people back into the Lord's presence.

25:21 The fourteenth to Mattithiah: Another of Jeduthun's sons (25:3). *Mattithiah* — 'gift of YHWH' — captures the singers' self-understanding: their voice was itself given to them, to be offered back in worship.

25:22 The fifteenth to Jeremoth: A son of Heman (25:4). The continuing roll testifies that worship in David's organized temple was not the work of a few stars but of two hundred eighty-eight trained singers (25:7) drawn from many households of Levi.

25:23 The sixteenth to Hananiah: The name means 'YHWH has been gracious' and is borne also by one of Daniel's three companions (Dan 1:6). Yahwistic names dominate the list, naming each singer's family theology at the level of personal identity.

25:24 The seventeenth to Joshbekashah: Heman's eleventh son (25:4). The unusual name belongs to a string of names David's spirit-led prayer-songs gave to Heman's sons (25:4-5), making the very list of names a hidden hymn of praise.

25:25 The eighteenth to Hanani: Heman's twelfth son (25:4). The full set of Heman's fourteen sons mirrors the broader pattern: each one personally named and assigned a course, no service treated as interchangeable.

25:26 The nineteenth to Mallothi: The list continues through Heman's house. The Chronicler's care to name every singer matches Paul's later insistence on the irreplaceable particularity of every member of the body (1 Cor 12:14-27).

25:27 The twentieth to Eliathah: Heman's son whose name means 'my God has come.' The hymnic resonance of the names continues — every name announced before the people becomes a brief confession in itself.

25:28 The one and twentieth to Hothir: Three lots remain. The numeric progression — 21st, 22nd, 23rd, 24th — has reached the point that ancient hearers would have begun to anticipate, knowing twenty-four courses must be filled (cp. 24:7-18 for the priestly courses).
25:29 The two and twentieth to Giddalti: 'I have magnified' (the Lord) — Heman's son whose name carries praise into the very enumeration. The list of names doubles as a chain of doxology.

25:30 The three and twentieth to Mahazioth: The penultimate lot. Mahazioth means 'visions' — a fitting name for the line of Heman, the king's *roeh* (seer; 25:5). The musical and prophetic gifts overlapped in this family's calling.
25:31 Romamti-ezer - Both these names belong to the same person. He is mentioned also 1 Chr 25:4. With this immense parade of noise and show, (David's own invention), Christianity has nothing to do.
